title: Chip-carved beech hand-mirror frame
subtitle: A decorative chip-carved frame for a small hand mirror
excerpt: A hand-mirror frame in seasoned beech, decorated with a border of chip-carved rosettes and six-point stars. The frame is bandsawn to shape from flat stock, then the chip-carved border is cut by hand. A project that combines simple joinery with surface decoration, resulting in a gift-quality piece.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for chip-carving border designs; Victorian-era craft manuals including Newey and Drage for frame construction.

content.text: Chip carving on a flat frame is the natural next step after chip-carved panels: the same three-cut chip is applied to a border design that runs around the mirror opening. Beech is the wood of choice for chip work; its close, even grain means the chip lifts cleanly without the edge feathering. The frame shape is cut with a bandsaw before the chip work begins; this is a surface-decoration project, not a sawmill project, and the bandsaw cut is a setup step rather than the main event. A small hand mirror insert; glass or acrylic; fits into a rebate at the back of the frame.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the chip-carving knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: the chip-carving knife is pushed or angled into the surface; the dominant motion is downward and angled, not lateral. Keep the off-hand clear of the cutting face.
Sharpening: the chip-carving knife must be razor-sharp. Strop every 20 minutes of work. A dull edge tears the chip wall rather than shearing it cleanly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable surface, good light. Chip carving requires the frame to be held steady on the bench; clamp or weight if the frame slides.
The wood and mirror
A piece of
seasoned

beech: 20 cm square, 16 mm thick, flat and free of knots. Cut the oval mirror opening 13 cm by 10 cm from the centre with a bandsaw or scroll saw. Cut a rebate 5 mm wide and 4 mm deep around the back of the oval opening to hold the mirror glass. A 13 by 10 cm oval mirror insert is a standard size available from craft suppliers.
Laying out the border design

Draw a grid of 10 mm equilateral triangles around the frame border in pencil. The border is 3 cm wide; three rows of triangles fit across it. The chip-carving design alternates six-point stars (six triangular chips arranged around a centre point) with single larger chips. Mark every triangle vertex clearly before cutting; the geometry drives the result.
Cutting the chips
The basic
chip cut
is three incisions converging at the deepest point of the chip. For each triangular chip:
Make a
stop cut
along each of the three sides of the triangle; pressing the chip-carving knife in at 65 degrees from the face, angled toward the deepest point of the chip. Three stop cuts define the chip boundary.
Make a slicing cut along the same line at a shallower angle (30 degrees from the face), meeting the stop cut at depth. The chip lifts free as this slicing cut reaches the stop cut.
Clean the chip floor with the tip of the chip-carving knife if the surface is rough. The floor should be smooth and angled evenly from rim to centre.
Finishing

Do not sand the chip-carved face; sanding rounds the crisp chip edges. Brush away dust with a soft brush. Apply two thin coats of Danish oil; the oil darkens the chip walls slightly and creates a subtle shadow that makes the pattern read more clearly. Apply the oil by brush, working into the chip recesses. Let each coat dry fully before applying the next.
Variations
Cherry or walnut frame: both take chip carving well. Walnut's dark colour makes the chip shadows less visible; cherry is a reliable alternative to beech.
Round mirror: the same border design adapts to a circular frame opening; the triangular grid follows the curve.
Care
Dust the chip surface with a soft brush rather than a damp cloth; water raised into the chip walls will lift any loose fibres. Re-oil once a year.

items.fix: Strop the knife. Re-establish the stop cut to the full 65-degree angle before attempting the slicing cut. A chip that tears means the stop cut has not fully defined the boundary.
items.cause: Chip-carving knife is dull or the stop cut was not deep enough
items.symptom: Chip floor tears rather than shearing cleanly

items.fix: On beech, the grain usually runs roughly parallel to the longer side of the frame. Cuts along the grain shear cleanly; cuts across or against the grain raise fibres. Plan the cut sequence so the clean-shearing direction comes last on each chip side.
items.cause: Cutting against the grain on one side of the chip
items.symptom: Chip walls have feathered or raised fibres
